While animal models have provided valuable insights, species differences in neuronal architecture and neurotransmitter systems necessitate direct examination of human epileptic tissue. We describe a comprehensive methodological framework for maintaining viable human cortical specimens obtained from surgical resections of epileptogenic foci in patients with medically refractory epilepsy. Brain slices (400-500 μm) were prepared within 30 minutes of surgical removal and maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 32°C. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using multielectrode arrays and patch-clamp techniques to characterize spontaneous and evoked epileptiform activity. Pharmacological interventions included application of various antiepileptic drugs, glutamate receptor antagonists, and GABA modulators. Tissue viability was assessed through cellular morphology, membrane potential stability, and metabolic indicators over 8-12 hour recording periods. Our protocol consistently yielded robust epileptiform discharges in 78% of specimens (n=45 patients), with maintained neuronal responsiveness to pharmacological manipulation for up to 10 hours post-resection. AMPA/kainate receptor blockade reduced seizure-like activity by 65±12%, while gabazine enhanced discharge frequency by 180±34%. This methodology provides a reliable platform for investigating human epileptic mechanisms and screening potential therapeutic compounds directly in pathological human neural networks, offering advantages over conventional animal models for translational epilepsy research.

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
abstract: Social hierarchies are fundamental organizational structures across mammalian species, yet the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. The mesolimbic dopaminergic system, critical for reward processing and motivation, has been implicated in dominance behaviors, but its specific role in hierarchy establishment and maintenance requires further investigation. Here, we examined the contribution of dopamine D1 and D2 receptors within the nucleus accumbens (NAc) and ventral tegmental area (VTA) to social rank determination in adult male C57BL/6J mice. Using a tube test paradigm to assess dominance relationships, we administered selective D1 (SCH23390) and D2 (sulpiride) receptor antagonists via bilateral microinjections prior to social encounters. Additionally, we employed optogenetic stimulation of VTA dopamine neurons during hierarchy formation trials. Behavioral analysis revealed that D1 receptor blockade in the NAc significantly reduced dominance scores (p<0.01), while D2 antagonism had minimal effects. VTA dopamine neuron stimulation enhanced dominant behaviors in subordinate animals (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis showed increased c-Fos expression in NAc medium spiny neurons following successful dominance displays. Microdialysis measurements confirmed elevated extracellular dopamine levels in dominant animals during social interactions. These findings demonstrate that mesolimbic D1 receptor signaling is necessary for establishing social dominance, while VTA dopamine release is sufficient to promote hierarchical status. Our results provide novel insights into the neuropharmacological basis of social hierarchies and suggest potential therapeutic targets for disorders involving aberrant social behaviors.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time measurement of dopamine release and uptake in brain tissue, yet comprehensive analysis of the complex kinetic parameters remains challenging. We developed DEMON (Dopamine Evaluation and Monitoring with Optimized Neurochemistry), a novel analysis software package designed to extract multiple kinetic measures from FSCV data with enhanced precision and automation. Using this software, we investigated cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling in nucleus accumbens core of urethane-anesthetized rats (n=8). Electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area (single pulse, 350 μA, 4 ms) evoked dopamine release, which was measured before and after systemic cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.). DEMON software automatically calculated peak dopamine concentration, rise time (10-90%), decay time constant (τ), and area under the curve while minimizing user bias through standardized peak detection algorithms and baseline correction procedures. Cocaine treatment significantly increased peak dopamine concentration by 312 ± 45% compared to baseline, prolonged the decay time constant from 1.24 ± 0.18 s to 4.67 ± 0.74 s, and increased total dopamine signal (AUC) by 586 ± 89%. Rise time parameters remained unchanged, consistent with selective inhibition of the dopamine transporter rather than altered vesicular release mechanisms. The DEMON software package provides researchers with a standardized, comprehensive tool for FSCV data analysis that reveals multiple dimensions of dopamine kinetics, facilitating more nuanced understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: [Regulatory molecular mechanisms of the neurochemical processes. History and the present time].
abstract: The understanding of regulatory molecular mechanisms governing neurochemical processes has evolved dramatically over the past century, from early observations of synaptic transmission to contemporary insights into complex signaling cascades. This review examines the historical development and current state of knowledge regarding molecular regulation in neurotransmission, focusing on key discoveries that have shaped modern neuropharmacology. We analyzed seminal publications from 1900-2003, tracing the progression from Dale's principle through the identification of neurotransmitter receptors, second messenger systems, and gene expression regulation. Historical analysis reveals three major evolutionary phases: the classical period (1900-1950) characterized by neurotransmitter identification, the receptor era (1950-1980) marked by pharmacological classification of binding sites, and the molecular biology revolution (1980-present) enabling detailed characterization of signaling mechanisms. Contemporary research demonstrates that neurochemical regulation involves intricate networks of G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, protein kinases, and transcription factors operating across multiple temporal scales. Key regulatory mechanisms include presynaptic autoreceptor feedback, postsynaptic receptor desensitization, synaptic plasticity through CREB-mediated gene expression, and glial-neuronal interactions. Recent advances in molecular biology techniques have revealed previously unknown complexity in neurotransmitter systems, including receptor heteromerization, epigenetic modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation. Understanding these multilevel regulatory mechanisms provides crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions in neurological and psychiatric disorders, representing a paradigm shift from traditional pharmacological approaches toward precision neuromedicine based on molecular pathophysiology.

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: The serotonergic system comprises anatomically and functionally distinct dorsal raphe (DR) and median raphe (MR) nuclei that exhibit differential responses to pharmacological interventions and may contribute differently to psychiatric disorders. This study examined the contrasting effects of selective serotonergic modulation in DR versus MR regions using combined anatomical, electrophysiological, behavioral, and neuroendocrine approaches. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received microinjections of the 5-HT1A receptor antagonist WAY-100635 (0.1-1.0 μg) into either DR or MR nuclei, followed by assessment of anxiety-related behaviors in elevated plus maze and open field tests, measurement of plasma corticosterone and ACTH levels, and single-unit recordings from hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed distinct 5-HT1A receptor distribution patterns between DR and MR regions. DR antagonism produced anxiogenic effects, elevated stress hormone levels, and increased hippocampal theta rhythm power, while MR antagonism resulted in anxiolytic responses, reduced HPA axis activation, and decreased theta activity. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated that DR manipulation primarily affected dorsal hippocampal neuronal firing, whereas MR modulation influenced ventral hippocampal activity. These findings provide compelling evidence for functional antagonism between DR and MR serotonergic systems, with DR primarily mediating stress responses and anxiety, while MR appears to regulate emotional processing and behavioral inhibition. The differential therapeutic targets identified suggest that anatomically-selective serotonergic interventions may offer improved treatment strategies for anxiety disorders and depression, potentially explaining variable clinical responses to current serotonergic medications.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: Brain energy metabolism is critical for normal neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain poorly understood in vivo. We developed an integrative computational model that combines real-time neurochemical measurements with metabolic flux analysis to characterize brain energy dynamics during physiological states. Using microdialysis probes in awake, freely-moving rats (n=12), we simultaneously measured extracellular glucose, lactate, glutamate, and GABA concentrations in the hippocampus during rest, mild stress, and recovery periods. Oxygen tension was monitored using polarographic electrodes, while local field potentials were recorded to assess neural activity. These experimental data were incorporated into a multi-compartment mathematical model describing neuronal and glial metabolic interactions, including the astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle and glutamate-glutamine cycle. The model successfully reproduced observed metabolic patterns, revealing that astrocytic glucose uptake increased 340% during neural activation while neuronal glucose utilization rose only 180%. Lactate production by astrocytes peaked within 2 minutes of stimulus onset, preceding maximal neuronal lactate consumption by 45 seconds. The model predicted that glutamate clearance accounted for 60-70% of total energy expenditure during high activity states. Sensitivity analysis identified astrocytic glucose transport and glutamate uptake kinetics as critical parameters governing metabolic responses. This integrative approach provides quantitative insights into brain bioenergetics and establishes a framework for investigating metabolic dysfunction in neurological disorders. The model's predictive capability may prove valuable for optimizing neuroprotective strategies and understanding energy-related pathophysiology.

title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
abstract: The American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP) has historically faced challenges in achieving equitable representation across gender, racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic dimensions within its membership and leadership structure. To assess current diversity metrics and identify barriers to inclusion, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of ACNP membership demographics, leadership composition, and meeting participation patterns from 2015-2020. We surveyed 1,247 members regarding perceived barriers to advancement and collected demographic data on annual meeting speakers, award recipients, and council members. Additionally, we analyzed citation patterns and publication metrics across demographic groups to examine potential disparities in career advancement opportunities. Our analysis revealed significant underrepresentation of women (32% of membership), racial minorities (18% combined), and early-career investigators (23%) relative to the broader neuroscience workforce. Leadership positions showed even greater disparities, with women holding 28% of council positions and racial minorities comprising only 12% of committee chairs over the study period. Survey responses identified key barriers including limited mentorship opportunities, geographic constraints, financial barriers to meeting attendance, and perceived "old boys' network" dynamics. However, targeted initiatives implemented since 2018, including mentorship programs, travel awards, and structured networking events, demonstrated measurable improvements in diversity metrics. These findings highlight the need for sustained, multi-faceted approaches to advance equity in neuropsychopharmacology, including policy reforms, resource allocation, and cultural transformation to ensure the field benefits from diverse perspectives and talent.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Schizophrenia patients demonstrate consistent deficits in facial affect recognition that significantly impact social functioning and quality of life. While these impairments have been well-documented behaviorally, the underlying neural mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current neuroimaging and psychopharmacological evidence to elucidate the neurobiological basis of affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia and evaluate treatment implications. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of neuroimaging studies examining affect recognition in schizophrenia patients compared to healthy controls, and analyzed clinical trials investigating pharmacological interventions targeting these deficits. Converging neuroimaging evidence demonstrates abnormal activation patterns in the amygdala, fusiform face area, and prefrontal cortical regions during affect recognition tasks in schizophrenia patients. Structural abnormalities in these regions correlate with behavioral deficits, suggesting a neuroanatomical substrate. While typical antipsychotics show limited efficacy for improving affect recognition, emerging evidence suggests that atypical antipsychotics, particularly those with significant serotonergic activity, may provide modest benefits. Adjunctive treatments targeting cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems show preliminary promise in small-scale studies. However, effect sizes remain modest and clinical significance unclear. Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia involve disrupted limbic-cortical circuitry, with the amygdala and prefrontal regions as key substrates. Current pharmacological approaches provide limited therapeutic benefit, highlighting the need for novel treatment strategies targeting specific neurotransmitter systems underlying social cognitive dysfunction.

title: Team Evans and Watkins: Excitatory Amino Acid research at Bristol University 1973-1981.
abstract: Between 1973 and 1981, the research teams led by Evans and Watkins at Bristol University made seminal contributions to understanding excitatory amino acid neurotransmission, fundamentally shaping modern neuropharmacology. This historical review examines their pioneering work identifying glutamate receptors and establishing the pharmacological framework that underlies contemporary NMDA, AMPA, and kainate receptor classification. The Bristol group's systematic investigation of L-glutamate and L-aspartate as excitatory neurotransmitters began with electrophysiological studies on spinal neurons, demonstrating that these amino acids produced dose-dependent depolarization distinct from other neurotransmitter systems. Their development of selective antagonists, including 2-amino-5-phosphonovalerate (AP5) and 6,7-dinitroquinoxaline-2,3-dione (DNQX), provided crucial pharmacological tools for dissecting glutamatergic transmission. Subsequent binding studies and receptor autoradiography revealed heterogeneous glutamate binding sites throughout the central nervous system, with particularly high densities in hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. The team's characterization of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) as a selective agonist and their discovery of the glycine modulatory site established the foundation for understanding NMDA receptor function in synaptic plasticity, learning, and excitotoxicity. These discoveries directly influenced the development of neuroprotective strategies for stroke and neurodegenerative diseases, while their receptor classification system remains the standard framework for glutamate pharmacology. The Evans-Watkins collaboration exemplifies how systematic neuropharmacological research can transform our understanding of brain function and provide therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
abstract: Addiction is characterized by a transition from impulsive drug-seeking to compulsive drug-taking behaviors, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenotypic shift remain poorly understood. We examined dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems in brain regions implicated in addiction using a well-validated rat self-administration model. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) underwent cocaine self-administration training followed by behavioral phenotyping using delay discounting and compulsivity tasks. Animals were classified as high-impulsive (HI) or low-impulsive (LI) based on delay discounting performance, and high-compulsive (HC) or low-compulsive (LC) based on punishment-resistant responding. Post-mortem neurochemical analysis was performed using high-performance liquid chromatography in the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. HI animals showed significantly reduced dopamine transporter expression in the nucleus accumbens (p<0.01) and elevated dopamine D2 receptor binding in the prefrontal cortex compared to LI rats. HC animals exhibited decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal striatum (p<0.05) and reduced GABA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex relative to LC animals. Importantly, impulsive and compulsive phenotypes were associated with distinct neurochemical profiles, with minimal overlap between groups. Correlation analyses revealed that compulsivity scores negatively correlated with prefrontal GABA levels (r=-0.67, p<0.001), while impulsivity measures correlated with accumbal dopamine transporter expression (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings suggest that impulsive and compulsive addiction phenotypes are mediated by dissociable neurochemical substrates, providing potential targets for personalized therapeutic interventions in substance use disorders.
